title: Dense Models
lesson:
A dense model is the plain, original shape of a large language model: every single one of its parameters (the learned numbers that make up the model) is used to process every single token (the chunk of text the model reads or writes). Nothing is skipped. If the model has 8 billion parameters, all 8 billion do work on every word, every time. For years this was the only shape anyone built, which is why "dense" wasn't even a word people used — it was just "a model". The name only appeared once a different shape, Mixture-of-Experts (MoE), arrived and people needed a way to say "the normal kind".

The whole orchestra plays every note

Picture a full symphony orchestra. In a dense model, every musician plays on every single bar of music — strings, brass, woodwind, percussion, all of them, the whole way through. The sound is rich and consistent, but you are paying every player's full salary for the entire concert even during a quiet passage that only really needs a violin. That is a dense model: maximum participation, maximum predictability, maximum cost. (Contrast that with MoE, where a conductor waves in only the few sections needed for each bar — we'll get there.)

A second analogy: the all-hands meeting

Imagine a company where, for every decision — big or tiny — the entire staff files into one room and everyone weighs in. You get the full collective brainpower on each question, and the process is dead simple to run: there's only ever one room and one routine. But it's slow and pricey, because you are paying and feeding the whole company to answer "what's for lunch?" just as you do for "should we open a new office?". A dense model treats every token like that all-hands meeting.

Why dense models are still everywhere in 2026

Despite the buzz around MoE, dense models remain the default for a lot of real products, for three down-to-earth reasons:

  • Predictable cost and speed. Because the work per token never changes, you can forecast latency (how long a reply takes) and memory use almost exactly. No surprises.
  • Easy to run anywhere. Most small language models you run on a laptop or phone are dense. Tools like Ollama and llama.cpp handle dense models with zero fuss.
  • Simpler to fine-tune. Techniques like LoRA and QLoRA were designed and battle-tested on dense models first.

The memory and cost reality

Because all parameters fire every time, a dense model's running cost scales almost directly with its size. The same rule of thumb from the parameters topic applies cleanly here — params x bytes-per-param tells you the VRAM (fast GPU memory) you need:

Dense 8B model, 16-bit:  8,000,000,000 x 2 bytes  = ~16 GB VRAM
Dense 8B model, 4-bit:   8,000,000,000 x 0.5 byte = ~4 GB VRAM
Every token: ALL 8B parameters do the math.

With a dense model there's no asterisk: the "sticker number" of parameters is also roughly the number doing work. That honesty is part of why people trust them for budgeting.

Dense vs MoE at a glance

Dense modelMoE model
All parameters active per tokenOnly a few "experts" active per token
Cost scales with total sizeCheaper to run than its total size suggests
Predictable, simple to serveFaster for the quality, but trickier to serve
Great on laptops/phonesUsually needs lots of memory for all experts

One trap worth naming early: in an MoE model, only a slice of parameters fire per token, but all of them must still be loaded into memory. A 100B-parameter MoE can be as fast as a 12B dense model yet still demands enough VRAM to hold the full 100B. A dense model has no such gap — what you load is what you use.

A quick code check

You usually can't tell dense from MoE by the name alone. The model's config file gives it away — MoE configs list a number of experts; dense ones don't:

from transformers import AutoConfig
cfg = AutoConfig.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B")
is_moe = getattr(cfg, "num_local_experts", None) is not None
print("MoE" if is_moe else "Dense")   # Dense

So the takeaway: a dense model is the "everyone shows up, every time" design. It costs more per unit of quality than the newer MoE shape, but it is simple, predictable, and friendly to small hardware — which is exactly why it's still the workhorse for on-device and budget-sensitive products in 2026.
mentalModel: A full orchestra where every musician plays on every bar of music. Rich and reliable, but you pay every player for the whole concert. In a dense model, all parameters do work on every token — no shortcuts, no skipping.
mistakes: Thinking "dense" is a special or fancy feature. It's the opposite — dense is the plain, original design. The word only exists to distinguish it from the newer Mixture-of-Experts shape.

Assuming bigger dense always beats smaller dense. Size is capacity, not skill. A well-trained dense 8B in 2026 can beat a poorly trained dense 70B from a few years back. Training quality matters as much as count.

Comparing a dense model's size to an MoE's total size. A 100B MoE may only fire ~12B parameters per token, so it can be as fast as a dense 12B. Comparing 100B-dense to 100B-MoE on speed is apples to oranges.

Forgetting dense cost scales straight with size. Doubling a dense model's parameters roughly doubles its per-token compute and memory. There's no free lunch like MoE's selective activation — plan your VRAM accordingly.
Believing MoE has made dense obsolete. Dense still wins for on-device and laptop use because it's predictable and memory-light per active parameter. Most models running on phones today are dense.

Mixing up "dense" the architecture with "dense" embeddings. A dense model means all parameters fire. A "dense vector" in embeddings means a list of numbers with no empty slots. Same word, totally different idea.
exercise:
Open the model cards of two well-known open models on Hugging Face: a dense one (e.g. a Llama 8B) and an MoE one (e.g. a Mixtral or Qwen MoE). For each, write down (1) the total parameter count and (2) the active parameters per token if stated. Then use params x bytes-per-param to estimate the 4-bit VRAM each needs to load, and note which number drives speed (active params) versus which drives memory (total params).

Stretch goal: Run the AutoConfig snippet from the lesson on three models of your choice and have it correctly print "Dense" or "MoE" for each. Then explain in one sentence why a dense 12B and a 100B MoE with 12B active might feel similarly fast yet need wildly different amounts of memory.
solution: Hint / worked answer: For the dense model, total and active parameters are the same number, so one figure drives both speed and memory. For the MoE, the active count (small) drives speed while the total count (large) drives memory — all experts must be loaded even though only a few fire. So a dense 12B and a 100B-MoE-with-12B-active feel similarly fast because each does ~12B parameters of math per token, but the MoE needs ~8x more VRAM because it must hold all 100B in memory. The AutoConfig check works because MoE configs expose a field like num_local_experts while dense configs don't.
summary: A dense model is the original LLM shape: every parameter works on every token — like an orchestra where all players play every bar. It's predictable, simple to serve, and friendly to small hardware, but its cost scales straight with size. Its counterpart, MoE, fires only a few "experts" per token to go faster, at the price of more memory and complexity.

keyTerms.def: A model where every parameter is used to process every token. The plain, original LLM design with no skipping.
The parameters that actually do math for a given token. In a dense model this equals the total; in an MoE it's far fewer.
A model split into many sub-networks ("experts"), where only a few fire per token — faster than its size suggests, but hungrier for memory.
A single number the model learned during training. A model is mostly a huge pile of these numbers.
The fast memory on a GPU where a model's parameters live while it runs. Roughly: params x bytes-per-param.
How long the model takes to produce a reply. With dense models it's very predictable because work per token never changes.
How many bytes each parameter uses (16-bit, 8-bit, 4-bit). Fewer bytes means less VRAM and faster inference.
The fixed wiring of a model — how its layers connect. "Dense" and "MoE" are two different architectures, not training choices.
